speaker: interviewer
text: This is Gienia Weinstein, tape number three, December 21, 1995. So tell me now what happened. You were talking about-- they took the blood from--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they let the girls go. And we have to go back to the barracks, you know? We go back to the barrack. This was in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, the same thing-- stay inside, they ask each other, when is this going to finish? When are we going to go to Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the men, they sent them to Gross-Rosen. He went and took them from Gross-Rosen. He didn't know It was 1,000 men-- 300 women. But he got the factory before Schindler. I didn't know Schindler before.

speaker: interviewer
text: He got the factory in-- not too far, in Krakow-Plaszow. Not too far. And what kind of factory he got? Aluminum factory-- it was aluminum.

speaker: interviewee
text: He got a lot of Jewish he works for him. And he was very close with the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then, the men came to Brinnlitz. They asked when the women? But they says, in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What he did-- this was a few weeks later-- he came to Auschwitz. Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schindler, by himself. He came. Everybody from the women on the list, they go to the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My God-- he was such a nice man. Too bad he passed away so young-- too bad. What he for the Jewish people in the world-- nobody. And he was an SS. I think the SS was, you know, covered. He made big business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came to Auschwitz and went down the list?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And everybody went to Brinnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: He put them on this special train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Special train, sure. And the men, they waited, they stayed, they looked over everybody that's here. But we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he just say they have to come? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they got a list. They know they have-- they have to let us out from Auschwitz. Otherwise, it wasn't so easy to go from Auschwitz. Or you go northern, or something else. We came to Brinnlitz. They saw us-- how we dressed. Nothing-- we didn't go-- we didn't have nothing. Yeah, in-- in Auschwitz, when we stayed from the morning-- 4 o'clock to 7:00-- an appell. Appell is they count everybody is in the-- in the barracks. They stay outside. It was so so cold. Nothing underneath-- just one dress and-- and the [NON-ENGLISH] shoes. Nothing more. It was so cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The winter was so bad. It was like now here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: '41. In '41. And when we stayed in the appell, the one girl came to me. And she says, I know you. The same town-- a small town. And she was in one barrack. She was a supervisor. She got everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: She said, you know what? Tomorrow I'll bring you some things to wear-- some stockings, I'll bring you. I have them, and I'll bring you-- and she did. She bring me-- Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. So many years-- no. I don't-- but I-- but I know who she is. And this was about three weeks. We were in Auschwitz about-- we slept up [NON-ENGLISH], like from here-- this table-- this table, there was 14 people. That 14 people-- this one pritch. Was so-- you couldn't move.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is a pritch? A bunk bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: A bed. They called it a pritch. It's 14 people. How can you live like this?

speaker: interviewer
text: But this was three weeks, so you can live. You have to. You tried your best. What can you do? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then when we came to Brinnlitz, we didn't get anything. I was sewing by hand, collars for the men's shirts. Sometimes they ripped, and by hand, I was sewing. And over there, they give me a piece of bread, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I got a one bread-- a big bread. It was 2-- 2 kilo-- 2 pounds-- kilo? Pound? Yeah. It was big in Poland-- they make the bread like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- every day I switched fresh-- and they gave me the fresh and I give them this. And I got for my husband, you know, and for myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In a pritch-- in another barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Brinnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brinnlitz. Sure. Where can you live? They can't give you a-- a room. No. It was lucky. But underneath was one woman. She was a other man's wife-- no, mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know-- the mother take this bread from the-- from her daughter. She was hungry, you know-- what can you do? They fight, they fight. The mother took from the daughter's bread. She was hungry. What can you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then was a factory in Brinnlitz from the wool. This factory got a lot of wool. And the factory didn't exist anymore. The people, they left. The men, they steal the wool in the night, and they bring to the women-- and they make sweaters. They make skirts. The one lady, she makes me a skirt and a jacket. I got for to wear, you know. And I gave them something. I don't remember whether bread or something. And I got, every day, something to do. I-- I was sewing-- after the work, we came to the barrack-- and I was sewing, you know, by hand. And I got some things to eat. And then, by-- by-- Schindler-- was-- the men, they worked on the big machines. They-- they-- they were for the military, you know-- I don't know how you call it. The factory-- they make everything-- of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ammunition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ammunition factory. Yeah, ammunition factory. The women, they stayed and they work, you know. They didn't got work to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They stayed. And-- we did some things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: What you can do? Until your dead, you know? And Schindler was supposed to become a commission-- became commissions off. All the woman has to do-- has to be here, and everybody has to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then Leipholtz, the Dutch, the-- the-- the German, the SS, he saw us. He says to Schindler, they don't have nothing to do. What do they do here? They stay? Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they did-- he says, no. It's not going to be like this. I will take all the women in the woods, and I'll kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leipholtz said this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: He wanted to kill you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He wants the kill us. Everybody. And Schindler heard this. He says to himself-- he was very close with the Jewish people. Very close, he told them-- this and this. So he says he has to go someplace. He went to the German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the front-- you know, the-- the-- military front. He says, he has to do something to him, because it's no good that he is by us here. He went to the German. He took the-- the-- he went, he says, this and this-- sent in papers that Leipholtz has to go in front where they fight. He came back in about a week or two. He has papers. He has to go in front, where they fight. And this was for us. We were saved. Otherwise, he would do this. He would kill everybody. This was Schindler-- what he did to us. Otherwise, nobody would be alive from the women. He saved us. It was a very big factory. Then one day, they bring a train with people-- so frozen and everything. The people, they fell from the train if they opened the door. And like-- like-- Muselmann. Muselmann was so skin and bones-- nothing else. Nobody wants to take them. It was 100 people, I think-- 100 people. He makes barracks. He says, I take them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. His wife was, too, over there. He made the barracks for these people. A lot of people was frozen to death, you know. She cooked for them, farina. You know what farina is? And she got as a small spoon-- she feed them like babies-- like babies. A lot of people survived from these people. And this was a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did that train come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows? Nobody let them in. Nobody. He says, I take them. And he took them. A lot of people survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help feed these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We saw them. And she did-- Schindler's wife. How can you go? You have to work in your place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: For Schindler?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed and worked. We didn't got nothing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ammunition. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you worked in the ammunition's factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- everybody worked in the ammunition factory. Everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then it was-- how many months I was over there? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Next year-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like this was in '41, '42-- the Germans, they came close to Brinnlitz, too. He was afraid, Schindler. And they looked for him. You know-- but he, before, he says to the Jewish people-- he got seven people.

speaker: interviewee
text: He left with these seven people. To-- I don't know-- Australia, someplace. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was the end. Now, before the-- it was the two German police. We walk a lot over there-- and they look of you, too. There was one police-- German police-- he watched you. And you got, every day, a piece of bread, like this. One was so terrible, he cut the head off, and took this bread-- and let them-- and let them-- and they [INAUDIBLE], where they went-- We went from-- from town to town. And came to-- when we was in Brinnlitz, and we hear the war is almost finished. So we take the two guys-- and the people, they kept one guy, they don't let them go.

speaker: interviewer
text: They stay, and they look like in prison-- you know? And Schindler says, the night before, this and this, the front is very close. The war is going to end. If you could see what's going on over there-- my God.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was crying. And everybody was happy and crying. They don't-- we don't have nobody. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Before he tell us, he got-- before he tell us that the war is over, but he got birthday about a month before. You should have seen what we did for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody throw him in the-- in the air. And everybody was so happy, you know? And we make those-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] everybody-- we got everybody's name in an album, wrote in Polish. Everybody's name he got, from all the people he saved.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a big kitchen. In the kitchen was big men, you know? And the German looked, and they says, what you think? The Russian coming? Don't wait for them. They're not coming here. We are here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then Schindler told us that the war is over. He left in the night with these seven Jewish people. He took the car, the wife, something else-- what he has to take. He left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which Jewish people? Do you know who they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they worked with us together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who they were? The names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name-- I remember one name-- Stern. I don't remember any more. Seven people got-- he was very close with them in the Emalia factory. Very close. They worked for-- for him, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came next day-- they looked for him, where he is. If would have been in Brinnlitz, they kill him. The Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who this is. I don't think--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was looking for him? Who was looking for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: For Schindler?

speaker: interviewer
text: But who was looking for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I know they were looking for him. All the Germans, they left what they was here. I have no idea. I know they looking for him. If they would be catch him, he would be killed. But he was so smart. He left with the Jewish people and he survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not how long. It's-- it's too bad. What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To who?

speaker: interviewer
text: You.

speaker: interviewee
text: To me? Everybody goes where they want to go. I was crazy-- I go to Poland. And I was-- and I says to my husband, maybe somebody is in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe it's not true. Who knows? And I stayed in Poland. The last one, they let us out-- So what happened? So do--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to Poland--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. It was over there-- a factory from-- a textile factory. Everybody got material for coats, you know, or something else. This is what they give you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we came to Krakow. No money, nothing. Where do you go? Where are you going to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to a-- a Jewish gymnasium. And the Jewish people had a big beautiful building. So we lived over there. We got one room, and lived over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and Berel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. And then my brother was-- came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sigmund?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sigmund. The other was killed. He came and was over there, too. He came. Then in a few days there was a pogrom. The Russian makes a pogrom  in military-- yeah-- In Krakow. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? Tell me what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, in that program, a lot of people was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see? What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they did?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came. I don't know. You know-- they did the things they don't have to do with the Jewish people-- it was Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw what they did. They-- they-- they killed some people. It was a pogrom for the Jewish people, you know-- the pogrom. They take you out from the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they make you line up?

speaker: interviewee
text: They go-- yeah-- they go inside. What they can-- they took it. You know? The Russians, they took it. But we were lucky we were alive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they kill someone you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This I don't know. They killed somebody, but I don't know who. There was so many people, you know-- it was a very very big building. And this building, they got the rooms-- who can-- they stay in-- I didn't got money. And Berel didn't got money. And they didn't give you money from the Jewish committee. They didn't give you nothing. I don't know how-- it was a long time. Then from Krakow, my husband, he got some Polack-- he used to come to him. He put the money, what he got, something he sells-- that he bring him from time to time, you know. And you didn't got nothing. You got nothing to start with something. After this, we were in Poland a long time. Was-- we got somebody to tell us to go to [NON-ENGLISH], you know. Over there there's a big factory. He started to work, I started to work in the factory. It was a very, very big factory-- 5,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the factory wasn't in one place. It was in different places-- small towns and big towns. I was together and my husband used to work for them. And he was so good-- the-- all the-- the presidents from the factory, they came, and they asked him what to do. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why he came here. He didn't work so hard his whole life like he worked here.
